title: Quantum Open Architecture: Everything Old is New Again
why:
- The article traces the evolution of modular, open computer architectures from systems like DEC minicomputers and Apple II to mixed-platform scientific instrument stacks, arguing that these fostered rapid innovation and customization for specialized scientific use.
- It highlights how open architectures enabled the integration of third-party hardware boards and components, creating a diverse ecosystem that facilitated scientific workflows, such as spacecraft data analysis at JPL and infrared astronomy on the Kuiper Airborne Observatory.
- The article connects these historical lessons to quantum computing, stating that the current shift toward quantum open architecture follows a proven pattern—using open interfaces and modular components to accelerate innovation and enable more practical scientific deployment.
- It provides concrete examples of open architectures supporting multiple vendors’ hardware and software in a single system, which allowed for flexible assembly of computing systems tailored to specific research needs.
